condition.qualitative_description: Cumulative forest-to-farm conversion has increased by more than 30 ha over the observation period, indicating progressive encroachment of agricultural activity into forest land.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: A positive trend in forest-to-farm conversion hectares is the most direct measurable indicator of encroachment. In semi-arid plateau and hilly terrain of Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka interior, and Odisha, forest fringe communities under livelihood pressure progressively clear forest margins for cultivation. Geospatial studies of forest fragmentation in Karnataka document reduction of evergreen-semi-evergreen forest cover from 57% to 32% over three decades, with cropland and built-up areas now accounting for 47% of formerly forested landscapes, directly reducing NTFP-bearing forest extent accessible to dependent communities.

condition.qualitative_description: Total urbanized area has expanded by more than 50 ha over the observation period, suggesting settlement encroachment into forest margins.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Settlement expansion into forest fringes reduces both the physical extent of NTFP-bearing forest and community access corridors. In semi-arid hard rock plateau regions, peri-urban growth along forest edges fragments contiguous forest patches, increasing edge effects and reducing interior forest habitat where high-value NTFPs such as tendu leaf, mahua, and amla are concentrated. A threshold of 50 ha accounts for meaningful landscape-level change at the micro-watershed scale relevant to village-level NTFP collection.

condition.qualitative_description: Village has a high Scheduled Tribe population (>30%) coinciding with measurable forest-to-farm conversion, indicating that forest rights disputes or displacement may be driving NTFP access decline for the most dependent communities.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Scheduled Tribe communities are disproportionately dependent on NTFPs for subsistence and income in semi-arid forest fringe areas of Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka, and Odisha. Where encroachment is occurring alongside high ST population share, the affected households are likely those with the greatest NTFP dependence and the least alternative livelihood options. Research on protected area conflicts in India documents that failure to recognize tenure rights under the Forest Rights Act results in displacement and loss of access for indigenous forest dwellers, directly reducing NTFP collection capacity.

condition.qualitative_description: Forest-to-farm conversion exceeds 100 ha over the record period in non-plains terrain, indicating substantial encroachment pressure in hilly or plateau landscapes where forest access routes are limited.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: In hilly and plateau terrain (terrain_cluster_id != 1, i.e., not mostly plains), forest-to-farm conversion above 100 ha represents significant fragmentation of contiguous forest patches. Geospatial fragmentation studies show that interior forest loss accelerates non-linearly once patch sizes fall below critical thresholds, reducing NTFP yields even in remaining forest areas due to edge effects, altered microclimate, and disrupted seed dispersal. The 100 ha threshold reflects a meaningful landscape-scale change for village-level NTFP catchment areas in semi-arid Deccan plateau contexts.

condition.qualitative_description: Many pending or rejected FRA claims with reported NTFP decline and forest conversion indicate tenure insecurity confirming encroachment vulnerability; high recognition weakens encroachment as primary driver.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Unresolved forest rights alongside measurable conversion corroborates encroachment as a tenure and access driver of NTFP decline.

condition.qualitative_description: Collectors report NTFP decline attributed to shrinking forest area or blocked/restricted access (not merely lower density inside intact forest). Decline greater than 25–50% increases severity.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Access- or area-attributed NTFP decline distinguishes encroachment from degradation or over-harvesting within intact forest.

overall_reasoning_note: Diagnosis of encroachment as the primary driver of NTFP decline should be confirmed by at least two signals: a positive trend in cd_forest_to_farm_ha (sig_01) is the strongest single indicator and should be present. Settlement expansion (sig_02) amplifies the diagnosis when co-occurring. High ST population share with concurrent forest conversion (sig_03) elevates severity and points to Forest Rights Act implementation gaps as a priority intervention. Sig_04 provides a magnitude threshold for landscape-level significance. If cd_forest_to_farm_ha shows no trend but NTFP decline is reported, consider alternative pathways such as over-harvesting, fire, or drought-driven forest degradation. In semi-arid hard rock plateau contexts, encroachment pressure is often driven by agricultural expansion during drought years when crop failures push households to clear forest margins for subsistence cultivation — check drought_severe_return_period to assess whether encroachment pulses coincide with drought cycles.

confounders.confounder: Forest degradation from fire or drought reducing NTFP yields without physical encroachment of forest land
confounders.how_to_distinguish: Check cd_forest_to_farm_ha trend — if flat or declining while NTFP decline is reported, fire or drought degradation is more likely than encroachment. Cross-reference with vegetation fire trend data; increasing fire frequency in semi-arid India has been documented by MODIS analysis and would reduce NTFP-bearing canopy without showing as forest-to-farm conversion in land use change data.

confounders.confounder: Over-harvesting or market-driven extraction pressure reducing NTFP availability without reducing forest extent
confounders.how_to_distinguish: If cd_forest_to_farm_ha shows no significant increase but NTFP decline is reported alongside high market demand or trader activity, over-harvesting is the more likely pathway. Encroachment is confirmed when forest extent itself is measurably declining, not merely NTFP density within intact forest. Ask community members whether forest boundaries have visibly shifted or whether collection areas have been fenced or cultivated.

missing_variable_questions.question_to_user: Have households in this village filed claims under the Forest Rights Act (FRA) for individual or community forest rights? If so, how many claims have been filed and how many have been approved or rejected?
missing_variable_questions.how_answer_updates_diagnosis: Low FRA claim filing in a high-ST village with documented forest-to-farm conversion suggests that tenure insecurity is driving encroachment — households without recognized rights may be clearing forest to establish de facto land claims. High rejection rates indicate institutional barriers to rights recognition that amplify encroachment pressure and reduce community incentive to protect forest boundaries.

choices.label: No FRA claims filed by community members
Claims filed but mostly pending or rejected
Several claims recognised (individual or community forest rights)

missing_variable_questions.question_to_user: Can you estimate how much of key NTFPs (e.g., tendu leaf, mahua, amla, bamboo) the community collected this year compared to five years ago? Has the collection area shrunk, or are yields lower even in the same areas?
missing_variable_questions.how_answer_updates_diagnosis: If collection area has physically shrunk (consistent with encroachment) rather than yields declining within the same area (consistent with over-harvesting or degradation), this strongly confirms the encroachment pathway. Quantitative decline in collection volume alongside reduced accessible forest area confirms both the stress and its cause.

choices.label: Little change — decline under 25% or stable collection
Noticeable decline of about 25–50% in collection volume
Severe decline over 50% or access largely blocked

sources.title: Geospatial analysis of forest fragmentation in Uttara Kannada District, India
sources.relevance_note: Documents forest-to-cropland conversion and fragmentation in Karnataka, showing reduction of evergreen-semi-evergreen forest from 57% to 32% over 34 years with cropland and built-up areas now at 47%; directly supports cd_forest_to_farm_ha as a diagnostic variable for encroachment in Karnataka interior contexts.

sources.title: Relocation from protected areas as a violent process in the recent history of biodiversity conservation in India
sources.relevance_note: Documents how failure to recognize tenure rights under the Forest Rights Act displaces indigenous communities from forest areas in India, directly reducing NTFP access for ST-dependent households; supports sig_03 rationale linking high ST population share with forest rights disputes.

sources.title: The Opportunity Framework 2020: Identifying Opportunities to Invest in Securing Collective Tenure Rights in the Forest Areas
sources.relevance_note: Provides global evidence that governments recognize legal ownership of only 10% of customarily managed land, supporting the diagnosis that FRA claim filing support is a priority intervention where encroachment and tenure insecurity co-occur.

sources.title: Trends in Vegetation fires in South and Southeast Asian Countries
sources.relevance_note: Documents statistically significant increasing fire trends in India (p=0.004) from MODIS data 2003-2016, relevant as a confounder distinguishing fire-driven forest degradation from encroachment-driven NTFP decline in semi-arid Indian forest landscapes.
